title: Woven baby blanket on a rigid heddle loom
subtitle: A soft, washable plain-weave blanket in superwash merino on a rigid heddle loom
excerpt: Weave a soft, washable baby blanket in superwash merino on a rigid heddle loom. The project covers warping the loom to the correct length and width for a standard cot blanket size, weaving a balanced plain weave, and finishing with a hemstitched or fringed edge.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from rigid-heddle weaving practice. Baby blankets are among the most common first rigid-heddle projects; the superwash merino specification and hemstitch finishing follow standard British guild workshop recommendations for washability.

glossaryTerms.definition: The simplest weave structure: each weft thread passes alternately over and under each warp thread. Also called tabby. The entire surface of a rigid-heddle blanket is plain weave.

The number of warp threads per unit of width, determining how densely the warp is set in the heddle. For a baby blanket in DK superwash merino, a sett of 10 to 12 ends per 10 cm gives a balanced, soft cloth.
An embroidery technique used to secure the weft threads at the start and end of a woven piece before cutting it from the loom. It prevents the weave from unravelling and produces a decorative border.

content.text: A baby blanket is an ideal first project on the rigid heddle loom: the
is forgiving, and the finished piece is immediately useful. Use superwash merino so the finished blanket is machine-washable on a wool cycle. Finish with
at each end for a clean, secure edge.

items.name: Superwash merino DK yarn, main colour
Superwash merino DK yarn, contrast colour (for stripes, optional)
Rigid heddle loom with at least 70 cm weaving width

content.text: For a finished blanket of 60 cm x 90 cm, warp to 65 cm width (allowing 5 cm draw-in) and 110 cm warp length (allowing 20 cm for take-up and tie-on waste). Set in a 10-dent heddle at 10 ends per 10 cm, giving about 65 warp threads total. Use a direct warping method: warp from front peg to back beam, working in the heddle holes.

Weave 2 cm of plain weave to establish the selvedge, then hemstitch across the width with a blunt darning needle before the weaving is cut from the loom. The hemstitch locks groups of 2 to 4 warp threads together to form a neat, stable edge.

Weave plain weave by alternating the heddle up and down positions, throwing the shuttle through each shed and beating each weft pick into place with the heddle. Beat consistently: the goal is a balanced weave in which the warp and weft threads are equally visible. Add stripe blocks in the contrast colour by switching shuttles at regular intervals.
Step 4: Hemstitch the end and remove from loom

When the weaving has reached 90 cm of woven length, weave 2 cm more, then hemstitch the end before removing the blanket from the loom. Cut the blanket from the warp beam, leaving enough warp ends to tie off securely or trim close to the hemstitch.
Hand-wash on a wool cycle or in cool water, press flat while damp, and allow to dry. The superwash merino will not felt and the blanket can be tumble-dried on low once fully dry.

items.problem: The selvedges draw in significantly, making the blanket narrower than planned
items.cause: The weft is being pulled too tightly across the shed before beating
items.fix: Leave a gentle arc of weft in the shed before beating down. Place the shuttle at a slight angle rather than pulling it straight through.

items.problem: The weave is unbalanced (warp dominant or weft dominant)
items.cause: The beat is too hard (weft dominant) or too light (warp dominant) for the chosen sett
items.fix: Adjust the beat firmness. A balanced plain weave at 10 epi in DK needs a moderately firm beat. Check balance by looking at the surface: warp and weft threads should be equally visible.
